summary: Portugal's biggest threat in the group (FIFA 13th) and a genuine dark horse under Néstor Lorenzo (27 wins in 45, just seven defeats). A multi-faceted attack led by James Rodríguez and Luis Díaz that can hurt teams through control or directness, and is lethal in transition.
style: A 4-2-3-1 / 4-3-1-2 — good at playing through pressure but happy to go direct to isolate Díaz (who 'makes difficult balls look simple'). Díaz drops into the left-back area to spring Mojica forward and free Jhon Arias from midfield. Out of possession an aggressive 4-4-2, with defenders and midfielders licensed to step out and win it high, then break fast.

counterReliance.note: Controls or goes direct; lethal breaking in transition

strengths: A multi-faceted attack (James + Díaz) that hurts teams multiple ways
Devastating transition — step out, win it, and release Díaz at speed (the goal vs Mexico)
James Rodríguez's set-piece delivery (3 of 5 Copa América 2024 assists from dead balls) to tall targets (Córdoba, Sánchez, Mina)
Díaz's elite club form carried into the national team
weaknesses: Ball-focused pressing can be manipulated by good teams (France exploited it in a 3-1)
Fall deep defending the box — leaves players free on the edge

keyPlayers.name: Luis Díaz
keyPlayers.note: 22 goals and 17 assists in 44 across the Bundesliga and Champions League; drives into the box, runs off the ball, and now thrives in midfield pockets

keyPlayers.name: James Rodríguez
keyPlayers.note: Creator-in-chief and dead-ball specialist — composure at high tempo and the set-piece supply

tactics: Play through or go direct to Díaz, manipulate defences with his drops to left-back, and break fast from an aggressive 4-4-2; punish from James' set pieces. With James and Díaz firing, the recipe could echo their 2014 run.
sources: The Athletic — 2026 World Cup tactical guide, Group K (5 Jun 2026)
John Walker (@johnwalker_1986) — pre-tournament tactical scouting, May–Jun 2026
